refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, reusable form. Atomicity is achieved by deferring the specific feature types (terrain, obstacles, zones) to sub-FRs.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent designated work zones (operational areas, restricted zones, safety perimeters) in the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and version the worksite model for access by planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety zone) queryable by planning/execution functions.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign feature capture responsibility to zone-specific acquisition modules; merge zone models via spatial registration and conflict resolution; expose unified model through a feature query interface.

DP3: Implement a real-time streaming pipeline that ingests raw sensor observations, applies incremental feature extraction and classification, and maintains a versioned spatial database; provide read-only snapshots and change notifications to downstream consumers.

DP4: Establish a declarative feature schema (geometry primitives, safety attributes, operational constraints) and enforce capture/representation conformance through schema validation; decouple acquisition logic from representation logic via an intermediate normalization layer.

DP5: Delegate feature acquisition to independent sensor subsystems (each with local processing); aggregate outputs via a central spatial reconciliation service that resolves conflicts, fills gaps, and publishes a canonical model; isolate reconciliation logic from sensor-specific details.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 + DP5 (Composite): Establish a declarative feature schema with safety and operational attributes; delegate acquisition to independent sensor subsystems with local processing; aggregate and reconcile outputs via a central spatial reconciliation service that enforces schema conformance and publishes a canonical, versioned spatial model queryable by planning/execution functions. Decouple acquisition from representation via a normalization layer.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor subsystems and reconciliation logic if sensor output formats are not normalized before reconciliation—mitigated by explicit normalization layer.
Coupling between schema evolution and downstream consumers if model versioning is not explicit—mitigated by versioned snapshots and change notifications.
Coupling between safety policy enforcement and feature representation if safety constraints are hard-coded in reconciliation—mitigated by externalizing safety rules to a policy layer.
Coupling between real-time acquisition and planning/execution if latency requirements are not specified—mitigated by clear SLA boundaries and snapshot semantics.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 + DP5 achieves loose coupling by: (1) separating schema definition from acquisition logic, (2) isolating sensor subsystems from reconciliation details, (3) providing a normalized intermediate representation, (4) publishing a canonical model with clear versioning semantics. Remaining risks are manageable through explicit interface contracts and policy externalization.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable or change infrequently during a planning/execution cycle. RISK: If policies change dynamically, the reconciliation service must support hot-reload of policy rules without disrupting ongoing model updates.

ASSUMPTION: Sensor subsystems can operate independently and produce outputs in a common coordinate frame (or can be registered post-hoc). RISK: If sensors have hard dependencies on shared calibration or real-time synchronization, independence is compromised; mitigation: establish a calibration service external to acquisition subsystems.

ASSUMPTION: Planning/execution functions can tolerate eventual consistency (i.e., model snapshots may lag real-time state). RISK: If safety-critical decisions require real-time guarantees, latency and freshness SLAs must be explicit and enforced.

ASSUMPTION: Feature classification and safety zone assignment can be automated or are pre-defined. RISK: If manual annotation or domain expert judgment is required, the acquisition pipeline must support human-in-the-loop workflows.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite geometry is sufficiently stable during a planning/execution cycle. RISK: If dynamic obstacles or temporary structures appear, the model must support incremental updates and change notifications.
ASSUMPTION: The 'agreed set of site features' is well-defined and documented. RISK: If feature scope is ambiguous or evolves, schema versioning and acceptance criteria must be explicit.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Latency and freshness requirements for the spatial model (e.g., 'model must reflect worksite state within 5 minutes'), (2) Scope of site features (e.g., 'static geometry, dynamic obstacles, safety zones, equipment locations'), (3) Tolerance for model incompleteness or uncertainty (e.g., 'model may have gaps in areas with poor sensor coverage'), (4) Update frequency and versioning semantics (e.g., 'snapshots published every 30 seconds; change notifications for safety-critical features'), (5) Integration points with planning/execution (e.g., 'query interface must support spatial range queries and feature classification filters'). Additionally, establish a separate FR for 'Enforce worksite safety policies during model reconciliation' to isolate policy logic from core spatial representation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial perception and reconciliation layer that owns the declarative feature schema, sensor abstraction interfaces, normalization pipeline, spatial reconciliation service, and canonical spatial model versioning. Coordinates with Vehicle and Safety Services via well-defined contracts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives raw sensor data streams from Vehicle subsystems (lidar, radar, camera, IMU); NGHE normalizes and reconciles these inputs into canonical spatial model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Publishes queryable canonical spatial model (versioned, schema-conformant) to planning/execution functions; enables downstream consumers to request spatial state without coupling to sensor acquisition.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits safety-critical spatial assertions (e.g., obstacle proximity, schema violations, reconciliation failures) to Safety Services for enforcement and alerting.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Optional: supports teleoperation queries and remote spatial model inspection if Teleoperation requests real-time perception state.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE owns the spatial perception pipeline end-to-end: schema definition, sensor abstraction, normalization, reconciliation, versioning, and canonical model publication. Sensor acquisition remains in Vehicle (appropriate boundary); safety enforcement remains in Safety Services (appropriate boundary). Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry ingress and Tasking/SafetyAlert egress cross the boundary, both well-defined contracts.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns sensor hardware and raw acquisition; it should NOT own reconciliation, schema enforcement, or canonical model versioning. Placing DP4+DP5 here would couple vehicle control logic to perception semantics, violate separation of concerns, and force Vehicle to manage multi-sensor fusion and schema conformance—responsibilities that belong in a dedicated perception service. High diffusion: planning, safety, and teleoperation would all need direct access to Vehicle internals.

Poor fit. Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting; it should consume spatial assertions, not produce or reconcile them. Placing DP4+DP5 here would conflate perception with safety logic, making Safety Services responsible for sensor fusion and schema management—outside its domain. High diffusion: Vehicle, planning, and teleoperation would bypass Safety Services to access raw spatial data.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a user interface and remote control layer; it should consume spatial state, not own perception infrastructure. Placing DP4+DP5 here would make Teleoperation responsible for sensor fusion and schema enforcement—a fundamental architectural inversion. Extreme diffusion: Vehicle, Safety Services, and planning would all need independent access to perception, defeating the purpose of a canonical model.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of DP4+DP5 because it is explicitly positioned as the perception and spatial reasoning layer. It sits between raw sensor acquisition (Vehicle) and downstream consumers (planning, safety, teleoperation). Centralizing the declarative schema, normalization, reconciliation, and canonical model versioning in NGHE ensures single source of truth, enforces schema conformance, and decouples acquisition from representation. This maximizes cohesion (all perception logic is co-located) and minimizes coupling (external modules interact via clean Telemetry/Tasking/SafetyAlert contracts).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is not yet established as a distinct module in the architecture, this recommendation requires explicit creation and staffing. Mitigation: Define NGHE as a dedicated subsystem with clear ownership and interface contracts.

Risk: Sensor acquisition in Vehicle may initially be tightly coupled to Vehicle control logic, making it difficult to cleanly separate raw telemetry streams. Mitigation: Establish a Vehicle Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) that decouples sensor drivers from control, emitting normalized Telemetry to NGHE.

Risk: Planning and Safety Services may expect direct access to raw sensor data for performance or safety-critical reasons. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE publishes low-latency, versioned spatial state with sufficient fidelity and metadata to satisfy downstream consumers without requiring raw sensor access.

Risk: Schema evolution and versioning may create compatibility issues if downstream consumers lag behind schema updates. Mitigation: Implement schema versioning and deprecation policies in NGHE; publish multiple schema versions concurrently during transition periods.

Risk: Reconciliation service may become a performance bottleneck if it attempts to fuse high-frequency sensor streams synchronously. Mitigation: Design reconciliation as an asynchronous, event-driven pipeline with configurable fusion windows and prioritization policies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP4+DP5 is necessary. The composite DP is well-articulated and maps cleanly to NGHE Solution. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion, recommend explicit refinement of the Vehicle-to-NGHE interface: (1) Define a Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) within Vehicle that normalizes raw sensor outputs to a common Telemetry schema before emission to NGHE. (2) Establish a Spatial Model Query API within NGHE that allows planning/execution and Safety Services to request spatial state by feature type, confidence threshold, and temporal window—avoiding ad-hoc direct access to internal reconciliation state. (3) Document the schema versioning contract: NGHE publishes schema version metadata with each spatial model update, enabling consumers to validate compatibility. These refinements preserve the DP logic while improving architectural clarity and reducing coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4+DP5 (composite) directly and sufficiently addresses FR: 'Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.' The DP specifies a complete solution path: (1) declarative feature schema with safety/operational attributes satisfies 'machine-readable model' requirement; (2) independent sensor subsystems with local processing satisfy 'capture'; (3) central spatial reconciliation service with canonical versioned model satisfies 'represent' and ensures availability to planning/execution. The decoupling of acquisition from representation via normalization layer is a sound design principle that reduces coupling and improves maintainability. DP logic is coherent and traces directly to FR.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) ownership boundary between Vehicle and NGHE is not yet explicitly defined. If SAL logic leaks into Vehicle control subsystems or NGHE acquisition logic, diffusion will increase. Mitigation: Formalize SAL as a distinct sub-module within Vehicle with clear responsibility for telemetry normalization only.

Moderate risk: Schema versioning and deprecation policy is mentioned but not yet operationalized. If downstream consumers (planning, safety) attempt to work around schema constraints by accessing internal reconciliation state, diffusion will spike. Mitigation: Implement and enforce Spatial Model Query API as the sole interface for schema-versioned access.

Low risk: Reconciliation service performance under high-frequency sensor fusion could create pressure to push fusion logic into Vehicle or Safety Services for latency reasons. Mitigation: Design reconciliation as asynchronous event-driven pipeline with configurable fusion windows; publish latency SLAs in interface contract.

Low risk: Teleoperation module may request real-time raw sensor access for operator situational awareness, bypassing NGHE canonical model. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE publishes low-latency spatial state with sufficient fidelity; document why raw sensor access is not necessary.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. Excellent fit: NGHE owns schema definition, sensor abstraction interfaces, normalization pipeline, reconciliation service, and canonical model versioning end-to-end. Sensor acquisition appropriately remains in Vehicle; safety enforcement appropriately remains in Safety Services. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking/SafetyAlert out, Network Link optional) are well-defined contracts with minimal coupling. No alternative module (Vehicle, Safety Services, Teleoperation) is suitable; all show poor containment (0.15–0.35) and high diffusion (0.65–0.85). Recommendation: Proceed with NGHE as owner; strengthen boundary clarity via explicit SAL definition and Spatial Model Query API formalization.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria are concrete and measurable: (1) 'Worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions' is verifiable via integration test: confirm NGHE publishes canonical spatial model to planning/execution subsystems with agreed feature set and schema conformance. (2) 'Agreed set of site features' is verifiable via schema validation: confirm NGHE enforces declarative feature schema and rejects non-conformant sensor inputs. (3) 'Worksite safety plan and operational policies' is verifiable via SafetyAlert interface: confirm NGHE emits safety-critical spatial assertions (e.g., obstacle proximity, schema violations) to Safety Services for enforcement. All three criteria map directly to NGHE IM responsibilities and can be validated via interface contracts and integration tests.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. FR allocation to NGHE Solution is unambiguous: NGHE owns the entire spatial perception and reconciliation pipeline (DP4+DP5). Upstream boundary (Vehicle Telemetry ingress) is clear: Vehicle acquires raw sensor data, NGHE normalizes and reconciles. Downstream boundaries (Tasking to planning/execution, SafetyAlert to Safety Services) are clear: NGHE publishes canonical model and safety assertions via well-defined contracts. No ambiguity about which module is responsible for schema enforcement, reconciliation, versioning, or canonical model publication. Ownership is concentrated and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Vehicle Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) as an explicit sub-module within Vehicle subsystem. SAL responsibility: normalize raw sensor outputs (lidar, radar, camera, IMU) to a common Telemetry schema before emission to NGHE. This decouples sensor drivers from Vehicle control logic and ensures NGHE receives clean, normalized inputs. Define SAL interface contract: input (raw sensor streams), output (normalized Telemetry with timestamp, confidence, feature type metadata).

Establish Spatial Model Query API within NGHE Solution as the sole interface for downstream consumers (planning/execution, Safety Services, Teleoperation) to access spatial state. API contract: input (feature type filter, confidence threshold, temporal window), output (versioned spatial model subset with schema metadata). This prevents ad-hoc direct access to internal reconciliation state and enforces schema conformance at query time.

Document schema versioning and deprecation policy in NGHE interface specification. Policy: NGHE publishes schema version metadata with each spatial model update; supports multiple schema versions concurrently during transition periods; deprecates old versions with 2-release notice. This mitigates compatibility issues and allows downstream consumers to validate schema compatibility before consuming spatial state.

Define reconciliation service latency SLA in NGHE interface contract. Specify maximum end-to-end latency from raw sensor ingress to canonical model publication (e.g., 100ms p95 for planning, 50ms p95 for safety-critical assertions). Design reconciliation as asynchronous event-driven pipeline with configurable fusion windows and prioritization policies to meet SLA without blocking Vehicle or Safety Services.

Add explicit acceptance test: confirm NGHE rejects sensor inputs that violate declarative feature schema and emits SafetyAlert to Safety Services. This validates that schema enforcement is operational and safety-critical schema violations are caught and escalated.

Add explicit acceptance test: confirm planning/execution functions can query canonical spatial model via Spatial Model Query API and receive schema-conformant results within latency SLA. This validates that DP4+DP5 satisfies the 'available to planning/execution functions' criterion.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The composite DP4+DP5 solution is logically sound, architecturally well-contained (0.92 containment, 0.08 diffusion), and verification is feasible with concrete acceptance criteria. However, the recommended_revisions identify four critical sub-system design decisions that must be decomposed to the next level to operationalize the solution and mitigate moderate diffusion risks: (1) Vehicle Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) formalization, (2) Spatial Model Query API as sole interface, (3) schema versioning/deprecation policy, and (4) reconciliation service latency SLA. These are not defects in the current FR-DP-IM mapping, but rather necessary architectural refinements that require explicit sub-FR derivation to ensure implementation clarity, interface contracts, and risk containment. Safety criticality (high) amplifies the need for explicit sub-level decomposition to validate schema enforcement and safety-critical assertion pathways. Proceeding directly to implementation without this decomposition risks diffusion creep and safety validation gaps.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Normalize raw sensor outputs (lidar, radar, camera, IMU) from Vehicle subsystem to a common, schema-conformant Telemetry format before emission to NGHE, ensuring decoupling of sensor drivers from Vehicle control logic and NGHE acquisition logic.

FR4.2: Provide a unified, versioned Spatial Model Query API as the sole interface for downstream consumers (planning/execution, Safety Services, Teleoperation) to access canonical spatial state, enforcing schema conformance and preventing ad-hoc internal state access.
FR4.3: Enforce declarative feature schema on all ingested sensor telemetry and reject non-conformant inputs, emitting SafetyAlert notifications to Safety Services for schema violations.

FR4.4: Operationalize schema versioning and deprecation policy, supporting concurrent multi-version schema compatibility during transitions and publishing schema metadata with each spatial model update.

FR4.5: Reconcile multi-sensor spatial observations into a canonical versioned model within defined latency SLAs (e.g., 100ms p95 for planning, 50ms p95 for safety-critical assertions) using asynchronous event-driven fusion with configurable fusion windows.

revise.why: No revisions to current FR-DP-IM mapping are required. All three gates pass. The recommended_revisions are architectural refinements that should be addressed via next-level sub-FR decomposition, not by revising the current level.

assumptions: Vehicle Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) can be formalized as a distinct sub-module within Vehicle subsystem without disrupting existing Vehicle control logic or introducing latency.
Spatial Model Query API can be implemented as a lightweight query interface without becoming a performance bottleneck for high-frequency planning/execution consumers.
Schema versioning and deprecation policy can be operationalized with acceptable overhead (metadata publishing, version negotiation) without impacting reconciliation latency SLA.

Reconciliation service can achieve target latency SLAs (100ms p95 planning, 50ms p95 safety) using asynchronous event-driven architecture without requiring synchronous blocking of Vehicle or Safety Services.
Safety Services can consume SafetyAlert notifications from NGHE schema enforcement with sufficient fidelity to make safety-critical decisions without requiring direct access to raw sensor data.

Downstream consumers (planning/execution, Teleoperation) will accept canonical spatial model fidelity and latency characteristics as sufficient for their use cases, without requiring raw sensor bypass.
open_risks: Moderate: SAL ownership boundary between Vehicle and NGHE could blur if Vehicle control logic attempts to access normalized telemetry for real-time feedback loops. Mitigation: Formalize SAL as unidirectional telemetry emission only; Vehicle retains raw sensor access for control, NGHE receives normalized telemetry.

Moderate: Schema versioning complexity could introduce compatibility bugs if downstream consumers attempt to work around schema constraints. Mitigation: Implement strict schema validation at Spatial Model Query API; document schema compatibility matrix and version deprecation timeline.

Moderate: Reconciliation latency SLA could be violated under high-frequency multi-sensor fusion scenarios (e.g., dense lidar + radar + camera at 30+ Hz). Mitigation: Design fusion pipeline with configurable fusion windows and prioritization; publish actual latency metrics and adjust SLA if necessary.

Low: Teleoperation module may request raw sensor access for operator situational awareness, creating pressure to bypass NGHE canonical model. Mitigation: Ensure NGHE publishes low-latency spatial state with sufficient fidelity; document why raw sensor access is not necessary.

Low: Safety Services may require real-time schema violation alerts with latency < 50ms, creating pressure to push schema enforcement into Vehicle or Safety Services. Mitigation: Design SafetyAlert emission as high-priority asynchronous event; validate latency SLA in integration testing.
evidence_needed: Integration test: Vehicle Sensor Abstraction Layer (SAL) successfully normalizes raw sensor outputs (lidar, radar, camera, IMU) to schema-conformant Telemetry format; Vehicle control logic does not access normalized telemetry; NGHE receives clean, normalized inputs.

Integration test: Spatial Model Query API successfully filters and returns versioned spatial model subsets; downstream consumers (planning/execution, Safety Services) can query canonical model without direct access to internal reconciliation state.

Integration test: NGHE rejects sensor inputs that violate declarative feature schema and emits SafetyAlert to Safety Services; schema enforcement is operational and safety-critical violations are caught and escalated.

Integration test: Schema versioning and deprecation policy is operationalized; NGHE publishes schema version metadata with each spatial model update; downstream consumers can validate schema compatibility before consuming spatial state.

Performance test: Reconciliation service achieves target latency SLAs (100ms p95 for planning, 50ms p95 for safety-critical assertions) under realistic multi-sensor fusion load; asynchronous event-driven pipeline does not block Vehicle or Safety Services.
Acceptance test: Planning/execution functions successfully query canonical spatial model via Spatial Model Query API and receive schema-conformant results within latency SLA.
Safety validation: SafetyAlert notifications from NGHE schema enforcement are sufficient for Safety Services to make safety-critical decisions; no raw sensor bypass is required.
